stem: A 3-month-old ex-term breastfed boy is brought to the GP for intermittent noisy breathing since 4 weeks of age that has slowly become more prominent. The noise is a soft inspiratory stridor that is worse when feeding, crying or lying supine, and almost resolves when held upright or in prone. He is thriving on the 50th centile, has had no fevers, no coryza, no preceding URTI, and feeds well with no choking or coughing. On examination he is alert, T 36.8 deg C, RR 36, HR 130, SpO2 98% on room air, with no recession at rest. Auscultation is clear. There is no barking cough and no hoarse cry. Examination of the oropharynx is normal, the cry is normal in pitch, and there is no haemangioma over the beard distribution. He has not had any feeding-related choking episodes and there is no history suggestive of an inhaled foreign body.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 deg C, RR 36, HR 130, SpO2 98% on room air.
data_table.Examination: Soft inspiratory stridor when supine, resolves prone or upright. No recession. Clear auscultation. Thriving on 50th centile.
data_table.Investigations: No fever, no preceding URTI. No cough, no choking with feeds.

lead_in: Which combination of most likely diagnosis and need for further investigation is correct?

options.text: Laryngomalacia; no investigation required if thriving with no red flags (no cyanotic spells, no feeding failure, no failure to thrive), with paediatric ENT referral only if red flags emerge
options.rationale: Positional inspiratory stridor starting in the first weeks, worse supine and with feeding and improving prone or upright in a thriving infant without red flags is the classic laryngomalacia picture, the most common cause of infant stridor. Australian practice is observation in the absence of red flags; flexible nasendoscopy and ENT referral are reserved for failure to thrive, severe feeding difficulty, cyanotic spells, or biphasic/atypical stridor, per RCH CPG: Stridor in children.

options.text: Subglottic haemangioma; arrange urgent MRI airway given biphasic stridor that worsens supine
options.rationale: Subglottic haemangioma typically causes biphasic stridor (not purely inspiratory) and may be associated with a beard-distribution cutaneous haemangioma; not consistent with this presentation.

options.text: Viral croup; commence oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg
options.rationale: Croup is acute with barking cough, hoarse cry and fever in 6 month to 3 year olds, not chronic positional stridor from the neonatal period.

options.text: Inhaled foreign body; arrange urgent rigid bronchoscopy
options.rationale: A retained foreign body presents acutely with a witnessed choking episode and focal asymmetry on auscultation, not chronic positional stridor.

options.text: Vascular ring; arrange CT angiography of the chest given positional stridor
options.rationale: Vascular rings cause biphasic stridor often with feeding difficulty and reflux, sometimes worsening with neck extension, and may need cross-sectional imaging; positional improvement in prone is more consistent with laryngomalacia and no concerning features are present.

explanation.summary: Laryngomalacia is the most common cause of stridor in infants. Soft, supraglottic tissue collapses inward on inspiration, producing positional inspiratory stridor worst when supine, feeding or crying, and improving when prone or upright. Symptoms usually appear in the first 2 to 4 weeks of life, peak around 4 to 8 months, and resolve by 12 to 24 months in the majority. Severe cases (failure to thrive, apnoea, severe distress) need ENT referral and possible supraglottoplasty.
key_points: Most common cause of stridor in infants
Positional: worse supine, better prone or upright
Symptoms begin in first weeks, resolve by 12-24 months
Refer if failure to thrive, apnoea, severe distress, cyanosis
Diagnosed clinically; flexible nasoendoscopy confirms
explanation.pearls: If the stridor is positional, gradual onset, with a thriving baby and no fever, it is laryngomalacia until proven otherwise. Acute febrile stridor with a barking cough is croup.

stem: A 7-week-old ex-39-week infant is brought in by her parents with a 3-day history of coryza, cough and wheeze peaking today. She is fully breastfed but over the last 12 hours has been taking about 40 percent of her usual volume and has had 3 wet nappies in 24 hours (usual 6 to 8). She is alert and pink with T 37.6 deg C, RR 58, HR 162, SpO2 91 percent on room air persistently. There is moderate intercostal and subcostal recession and widespread fine inspiratory crackles plus expiratory wheeze. There have been no apnoeic episodes witnessed by the parents or in the department. She is fully immunised for age. There are no risk factors for severe disease beyond her young age. Capillary refill is 2 seconds.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.6 deg C, RR 58, HR 162, SpO2 91% on room air.
data_table.Examination: Alert, pink. Moderate recession. Widespread fine crackles and wheeze. Capillary refill 2 sec.
data_table.Investigations: Feeding 40% of normal, 3 wet nappies in 24 hours. No apnoea witnessed.

lead_in: Which combination of features most clearly satisfies RCH admission criteria and dictates immediate disposition?

options.text: Respiratory rate of 58 per minute alone; admit for observation
options.rationale: An RR of 58 in bronchiolitis is expected and not by itself an admission threshold under RCH CPG; admission depends on SpO2, feeding, apnoea, severe work of breathing or social factors.

options.text: Persistent SpO2 91 percent on room air plus feeding under 50 percent of normal; admit for supplemental oxygen and feeding support
options.rationale: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis specifies admission when SpO2 is persistently under 92 percent on room air, feeding is under 50 percent of normal, there is apnoea, severe work of breathing or social concerns. This infant meets both the SpO2 and feeding thresholds; admission is indicated for low-flow oxygen and nasogastric or IV hydration.

options.text: Temperature 37.6 deg C with a sibling URTI; admit for sepsis workup
options.rationale: Low-grade fever and household viral contact are typical features of bronchiolitis and do not meet admission criteria nor trigger a sepsis workup in a stable child over 6 weeks.

options.text: Heart rate 162 with no other abnormality; admit for telemetry
options.rationale: Tachycardia of 162 is appropriate for fever and respiratory effort at this age and is not by itself an admission threshold.

options.text: Wheeze and crackles without hypoxaemia or feeding compromise; admit for nebulised salbutamol
options.rationale: Nebulised salbutamol is not recommended in bronchiolitis under 12 months; auscultatory findings alone do not meet admission criteria.

explanation.summary: RCH admission criteria for bronchiolitis include SpO2 persistently below 92% on room air, feeding under 50% of normal, apnoea (witnessed or risk factors), severe respiratory distress, dehydration, or social concerns. Management is supportive: minimal handling, NG or IV hydration, low-flow O2 to keep SpO2 at or above 92%, and HFNC if escalation needed.
key_points: SpO2 <92% on room air = admit
Feeding <50% of normal over preceding 24 hours = admit
Apnoea or risk factors for apnoea (under 6 weeks, ex-premature) = admit
Salbutamol, steroids, antibiotics and chest physio are not routinely indicated
Discharge once feeding adequately and SpO2 stable in air
explanation.pearls: In infants under 6 weeks always ask specifically about apnoea or pauses in breathing - they may be the only sign of severe disease.

stem: A 6-year-old boy with a 2-year diagnosis of asthma on PRN salbutamol presents in winter with worsening wheeze over 6 hours despite three sets of 6 puffs of salbutamol via spacer at home. He has had two ED presentations in the last year and no ICU admissions. On arrival he is too breathless to speak in full sentences, RR 44, HR 142, SpO2 90 percent on room air. There is marked accessory muscle use with tracheal tug and intercostal recession, widespread polyphonic wheeze with prolonged expiratory phase and only moderate air entry bilaterally. He is alert but anxious. PEFR cannot be obtained. He has had no recent oral steroid courses, no fever, and no focal chest signs. Chest examination shows no asymmetry to suggest foreign body or pneumothorax.

data_table.Vital signs: RR 44, HR 142, SpO2 90% room air, T 37.0 deg C.
data_table.Examination: Speaks in phrases. Marked accessory muscle use. Widespread wheeze, moderate air entry. Alert.
data_table.Investigations: PEFR unobtainable. No focal signs. No fever.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate first-line therapy package?

options.text: Continuous nebulised salbutamol 5 mg, ipratropium 250 microgram added to each of the first three nebs, oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg (maximum 50 mg) as soon as possible, controlled supplemental oxygen titrated to SpO2 over 94 percent
options.rationale: This is severe acute asthma by Australian severity criteria (unable to speak in sentences, SpO2 90 percent, marked work of breathing). National Asthma Council and RCH guidance: continuous or back-to-back nebulised salbutamol, ipratropium added to first three nebs, oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg immediately, and titrated oxygen. IV magnesium is the next step if no response.
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

options.text: Salbutamol 6 puffs via spacer every 20 minutes for one hour; withhold steroids until registrar review
options.rationale: MDI-spacer is for mild-moderate disease; severe disease with SpO2 90 percent requires nebulised therapy plus immediate oral steroid.

options.text: IV magnesium sulphate 50 mg/kg over 20 minutes and IV hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg, with no inhaled therapy
options.rationale: IV magnesium is for asthma not responding to first-line inhaled bronchodilator plus oral steroid; bypassing inhaled therapy is not standard.
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 25 mg/kg, oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg, nebulised salbutamol every 4 hours as required
options.rationale: Antibiotics are not routine in acute asthma without focal chest signs or fever, and 4-hourly nebs are inadequate for severe disease.

options.text: Nebulised adrenaline 5 mg, oral dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg, observe for 4 hours
options.rationale: Nebulised adrenaline is for croup; oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg is the standard steroid in paediatric acute asthma.

explanation.summary: Severity in paediatric asthma is graded clinically by ability to talk, work of breathing, SpO2 and mental state. Severe asthma is treated with continuous nebulised salbutamol, ipratropium added to the first three nebs, oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg (max 50 mg) and supplemental oxygen to keep SpO2 at or above 94%. PEFR is not used in young children to triage.
key_points: Mild-moderate: salbutamol via MDI-spacer (6-12 puffs), oral prednisolone if moderate
Severe: continuous salbutamol nebs + ipratropium x3 + oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg
Critical (silent chest, exhaustion, cyanosis, drowsy): IV magnesium, ICU referral
Antibiotics only if focal infection
Reassess after each intervention; trend rather than single snapshot
explanation.pearls: Speaking in phrases or single words and SpO2 below 92% on room air should immediately trigger nebulised therapy plus oral steroid in any child with known asthma.

sources.label: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

stem: An 8-year-old with mild persistent asthma attends a routine GP review. His mother reports symptoms remain uncontrolled despite using salbutamol via MDI with a mouthpiece spacer for 12 months. Adherence is good. On observed technique he shakes the canister, attaches it to the spacer, places the mouthpiece in his mouth, fires four puffs into the spacer in rapid succession, then takes two quick breaths without sealing his lips around the mouthpiece between actuations. He has no oromotor or developmental concerns and is growing on the 50th centile. He is asymptomatic with normal spirometry (FEV1 92 percent predicted, FEV1/FVC 0.84) at review.

data_table.Vital signs: Asymptomatic at review. T 36.7 deg C, RR 18, HR 92, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Investigations: Spirometry FEV1 92% predicted, FEV1/FVC 0.84 (within normal limits).

lead_in: Which is the single most important correction to make to his spacer technique?

options.text: Switch from a mouthpiece spacer to a face-mask spacer to maximise drug delivery for his age
options.rationale: Face-mask spacers are preferred only when a child cannot reliably seal lips on a mouthpiece, typically under 4 years; at 8 the mouthpiece is preferred when used correctly.
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

options.text: Wash the spacer with hot soapy water before each use and dry it with a clean towel
options.rationale: Spacers are washed monthly with detergent and AIR-dried (no towel) to preserve antistatic properties; towel-drying creates static that reduces drug delivery.
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

options.text: Hold the breath for 30 seconds after each inhalation to maximise small-airway deposition
options.rationale: Breath-hold of about 5-10 seconds is recommended; 30 seconds offers no additional benefit and is impractical.
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

options.text: Fire one puff at a time, take 4 tidal breaths (or one slow deep breath with 5-second breath-hold) per puff, and maintain a lip seal on the mouthpiece throughout
options.rationale: The two most common technique errors are firing multiple puffs without separating them (drug coalesces and impacts on the spacer wall) and breaking the lip seal between breaths. The single most important correction is one puff per actuation with 4 tidal breaths and a maintained lip seal, per the National Asthma Council How to Use Your Spacer factsheet.
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

options.text: Replace the MDI-spacer with a dry-powder inhaler now that he is old enough to follow staged instructions
options.rationale: DPIs require a fast inspiratory flow that not all 8-year-olds reliably generate, especially during acute asthma; properly used MDI plus spacer is preferred.
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

explanation.summary: Correct MDI-spacer technique: shake canister, attach, lip-seal on the mouthpiece, fire ONE puff, then either 4 slow tidal breaths or one slow deep breath followed by a 5-second breath-hold, then repeat for the next puff. Wash monthly with detergent, drip-dry, do not towel-dry.
key_points: One puff at a time - multiple puffs reduce delivery
Tidal breathing (younger) or slow deep breath + breath-hold (older)
Tight lip seal on mouthpiece
Air-dry after monthly wash (preserves antistatic coating)
Mask under 4 years, mouthpiece preferred from age 4 onwards if technique is good
explanation.pearls: If asthma is uncontrolled, watch the patient use their inhaler before changing medication. Most apparent treatment failure is technique failure.

sources.label: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

stem: A 5-week-old ex-34-week infant is brought to the resuscitation bay at a tertiary paediatric ED with day 4 of an RSV-positive bronchiolitis. Over the last hour she has had two witnessed apnoeic pauses of 20 seconds each with bradycardia to 80 and desaturation to 78% requiring tactile stimulation to recover. On arrival she has RR 70, HR 184, SpO2 89% on 2 L/min nasal prong oxygen, T 37.3 deg C. She has marked subcostal and intercostal recession with head bobbing, nasal flaring and grunting. Air entry is fair bilaterally with widespread fine crackles and wheeze. She is mottled and her capillary refill is 3 seconds peripherally. She is lethargic but rousable. A venous gas shows pH 7.28, pCO2 65 mmHg, HCO3 24 mmol/L, lactate 2.1 mmol/L. NG feeds were started 12 hours ago and have been tolerated at 50% volume. The PICU registrar is at the bedside. She is fully immunised and weighs 3.6 kg.

data_table.Vital signs: RR 70, HR 184, SpO2 89% on 2 L O2, T 37.3 deg C.
data_table.Examination: Marked recession, head bobbing, grunting. Mottled, CRT 3 sec. Lethargic but rousable.
data_table.Investigations: VBG: pH 7.28, pCO2 65 mmHg, HCO3 24, lactate 2.1. RSV PCR positive. Two apnoeas with bradycardia in last hour.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate next step in respiratory support?

options.text: Increase nasal prong O2 to 4 L/min and continue NG feeds
options.rationale: Low-flow O2 will not unload the respiratory muscles or prevent further apnoeic episodes; this infant has type 2 respiratory failure and ongoing apnoea (RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis).

options.text: Commence nebulised hypertonic saline 3% 4 mL six-hourly with nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg
options.rationale: Hypertonic saline and salbutamol are not recommended for routine bronchiolitis management in Australian guidelines and do nothing for impending respiratory failure (RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis; PREDICT bronchiolitis guideline).

options.text: Start high-flow nasal cannula at 2 L/kg/min, titrate FiO2 to SpO2 92-96%
options.rationale: HFNC at 2 L/kg/min is the first escalation step for severe bronchiolitis with respiratory failure or apnoea, supported by the PARIS trial and RCH CPG. CPAP or intubation is reserved for ongoing apnoea, rising CO2 or exhaustion despite optimal HFNC. With recurrent apnoea and a pCO2 of 65 mmHg, HFNC is initiated now with clear escalation criteria (RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis; Franklin PARIS NEJM 2018).

options.text: Intubate immediately with rapid sequence induction and start mechanical ventilation now
options.rationale: Intubation is appropriate for ongoing apnoea or exhaustion despite non-invasive support, persistent hypoxia, or progressive hypercapnia despite HFNC or CPAP; it is not the first escalation step from low-flow oxygen (RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis).

options.text: Administer IV dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg and a single dose of IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg
options.rationale: Steroids and antibiotics are not indicated for uncomplicated viral bronchiolitis and do not address the immediate respiratory failure (RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis; Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Severe bronchiolitis with apnoea, hypercapnia or persistent hypoxia despite low-flow O2 is treated with stepwise escalation: low-flow nasal prong O2 -> HFNC 2 L/kg/min -> CPAP -> intubation. HFNC is now the first non-invasive step (PARIS trial), reserving CPAP and intubation for failure. Antibiotics and salbutamol are not routinely indicated.
key_points: HFNC flow rate 2 L/kg/min, FiO2 titrated to SpO2 92-96%
Indications to escalate: ongoing apnoea, rising CO2, exhaustion, FiO2 >60%
CPAP useful for type 2 respiratory failure not responding to HFNC
Intubation for failure of non-invasive support
Salbutamol, steroids, antibiotics: not routine
explanation.pearls: Recurrent apnoea in a young infant is by itself a reason to escalate from low-flow O2 to HFNC or CPAP, even without severe hypoxia.

stem: A 7-year-old girl with known asthma has been in the paediatric ED for 90 minutes with a severe exacerbation. She has received continuous nebulised salbutamol 5 mg with ipratropium 250 microg added to the first three nebs, plus oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg administered at arrival. Despite this she continues to speak only in single words, RR 42, HR 154, SpO2 90% on 6 L Hudson mask, with marked accessory muscle use and a prolonged expiratory phase. There has been no improvement in her wheeze, work of breathing or air entry over the last 30 minutes. She has had two previous PICU admissions for asthma but has never been intubated. She remains alert and oriented. She has a patent IV cannula. Her venous gas shows pH 7.34, pCO2 38 mmHg, HCO3 19 mmol/L, lactate 4.8 mmol/L (attributed to high-dose salbutamol). PICU has been contacted.

data_table.Vital signs: RR 42, HR 154, SpO2 90% on 6 L Hudson mask, T 36.9 deg C.
data_table.Examination: Single-word speech. Marked accessory use. Prolonged expiration. Air entry reduced. Alert.
data_table.Investigations: VBG: pH 7.34, pCO2 38, HCO3 19, lactate 4.8. CXR: hyperinflation, no focal consolidation, no pneumothorax.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next pharmacological step?

options.text: IV aminophylline loading dose 10 mg/kg over 60 minutes followed by infusion
options.rationale: Aminophylline is a third-line option after IV magnesium has been trialled or in PICU; its therapeutic index is narrow and toxicity (arrhythmia, seizure) is common (RCH CPG: Asthma acute; AMH Children's Dosing Companion).

options.text: Nebulised adrenaline 5 mg every 20 minutes for three doses, no IV therapy
options.rationale: Nebulised adrenaline is indicated in croup, not asthma; in asthma it offers no additional benefit over selective beta-agonists (RCH CPG: Asthma acute).

options.text: IV salbutamol bolus 15 microg/kg over 10 minutes followed by an infusion at 1 microg/kg/min
options.rationale: IV salbutamol can be used in critical asthma but is usually after IV magnesium has failed and carries risk of lactic acidosis and tachyarrhythmia; the existing lactate of 4.8 already reflects beta-agonist load (RCH CPG: Asthma acute).

options.text: IV hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg in addition to the oral prednisolone already given at arrival
options.rationale: Oral prednisolone has equivalent efficacy to IV hydrocortisone in children who can tolerate oral; doubling up does not provide additional benefit (RCH CPG: Asthma acute; National Asthma Council Handbook).

options.text: IV magnesium sulphate 50 mg/kg (maximum 2 g) over 20 minutes now
options.rationale: IV magnesium 50 mg/kg (max 2 g) over 20 minutes is the recommended second-line bronchodilator for paediatric asthma not responding to first-line inhaled therapy plus oral steroid, with PICU involvement; aminophylline and IV salbutamol are reserved for further failure (RCH CPG: Asthma acute; National Asthma Council Handbook).
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

explanation.summary: When severe paediatric asthma fails to respond to first-line therapy (continuous salbutamol nebs, ipratropium, oral prednisolone) after 30 to 60 minutes, the next step is IV magnesium sulphate 50 mg/kg (max 2 g) over 20 minutes with PICU involvement. Further failure prompts IV salbutamol or aminophylline infusions and consideration of intubation.
key_points: First-line: continuous nebs + ipratropium x3 + oral prednisolone
Second-line: IV magnesium 50 mg/kg over 20 min, max 2 g
Third-line: IV salbutamol or aminophylline infusion (PICU)
Watch for salbutamol lactic acidosis, hypokalaemia, tachyarrhythmia
Intubation is high-risk in asthma; use experienced operator and ketamine
explanation.pearls: A normal or rising pCO2 in a child with severe asthma is an ominous sign of impending respiratory failure - it should escalate, not reassure.

sources.label: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

stem: A 4-year-old previously well girl presents to a regional ED with 3 days of fever to 39.2 deg C, productive cough and reduced oral intake. She is fully immunised including 13-valent pneumococcal conjugate vaccine. On examination she is alert and interactive, T 38.9 deg C, RR 38, HR 130, SpO2 95% on room air, with mild subcostal recession. There are focal crackles and bronchial breath sounds at the right base with reduced air entry, and no wheeze. Capillary refill is 2 seconds and she is feeding 70% of her normal volume. She has not had vomiting and is tolerating sips of oral fluid in the department. CXR shows a right lower lobe consolidation with no effusion and no pneumatocoele. CRP is 92 mg/L. She has no penicillin allergy and weighs 16 kg. Local antimicrobial sensitivities show no significant pneumococcal resistance.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.9 deg C, RR 38, HR 130, SpO2 95% room air.
data_table.Examination: Alert. Mild recession. Right basal focal crackles + bronchial breathing. No wheeze. CRT 2 sec.
data_table.Investigations: CXR: RLL consolidation, no effusion, no pneumatocoele. CRP 92. Feeding 70% normal.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line antibiotic?

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 25 mg/kg every 8 hours for 5 days
options.rationale: Therapeutic Guidelines recommend oral amoxicillin 25 mg/kg every 8 hours for 5 days as first-line for uncomplicated paediatric CAP in a child well enough for oral therapy and able to tolerate oral intake; Streptococcus pneumoniae remains the most likely pathogen and is well covered (Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory; RCH CPG: CAP).

options.text: Oral azithromycin 10 mg/kg daily for 5 days as monotherapy
options.rationale: Azithromycin (a macrolide) is reserved for suspected atypical pneumonia (older children with subacute, dry cough, extrapulmonary features) or as add-on; lobar consolidation in a 4-year-old is most consistent with pneumococcal disease (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg daily for 5 days
options.rationale: IV antibiotics are reserved for children unable to tolerate oral therapy, severe disease (hypoxia <92%, severe distress) or complicated pneumonia (effusion, empyema); this child meets criteria for oral therapy (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 22.5 mg/kg every 12 hours for 7 days
options.rationale: Amoxicillin-clavulanate adds beta-lactamase cover that is not required for typical pneumococcal CAP; it is reserved for aspiration pneumonia, complicated CAP or failure of amoxicillin (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Oral flucloxacillin 12.5 mg/kg every 6 hours for 10 days
options.rationale: Flucloxacillin targets Staphylococcus aureus and is added when staphylococcal pneumonia is suspected (cavitations, pneumatocoeles, severe necrotising disease), not first-line for uncomplicated lobar CAP (Therapeutic Guidelines; RCH CPG: CAP).

explanation.summary: Uncomplicated paediatric CAP in a child tolerating oral intake without hypoxia is treated with oral amoxicillin 25 mg/kg every 8 hours for 5 days. IV therapy is reserved for severe disease (SpO2 <92%, severe distress, vomiting, dehydration) or complicated pneumonia. Atypical cover (azithromycin) is added when school-age children present with subacute or extrapulmonary features.
key_points: First-line oral: amoxicillin 25 mg/kg q8h x 5 days
Indications for IV: SpO2 <92%, severe distress, vomiting, complicated CAP
Atypical cover: azithromycin, especially older children with subacute course
Effusion or empyema: chest USS + drainage and IV antibiotics
Routine CXR is not needed for uncomplicated CAP but useful here for severity
explanation.pearls: Most CAP in vaccinated children is treated outpatient. Reserve broad-spectrum and IV options for clearly defined severity criteria, not parental anxiety.

stem: A 5-year-old boy presents to the GP after his mother noticed 4 days of cough, fever to 39.0 deg C, and lethargy. He is fully immunised. His older brother has a coryzal illness with low-grade fever. On examination he is flushed, T 38.7 deg C, RR 36, HR 128, SpO2 96% on room air. Auscultation reveals focal bronchial breathing and end-inspiratory crackles at the left upper zone with normal findings elsewhere. He is mildly tachypnoeic but has no recession. CXR (obtained earlier at the request of an after-hours GP) shows a dense lobar consolidation of the left upper lobe with air bronchograms and no effusion. CRP is 124 mg/L, white cell count 18.2 x10^9/L with neutrophilia, and procalcitonin is 2.4 microg/L. He is feeding adequately and the GP needs to decide whether the picture supports bacterial or viral aetiology before prescribing.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.7 deg C, RR 36, HR 128, SpO2 96% room air.
data_table.Examination: Focal LUZ bronchial breathing and crackles. No recession. Otherwise normal.
data_table.Investigations: CXR: dense LUL lobar consolidation with air bronchograms. CRP 124 mg/L. WCC 18.2 with neutrophilia. Procalcitonin 2.4 microg/L.

lead_in: Which finding most strongly favours a bacterial (typically pneumococcal) aetiology over a viral cause?

options.text: Respiratory rate persistently around 36 breaths per minute today
options.rationale: Tachypnoea is a non-specific marker of disease severity present in both bacterial and viral pneumonia and does not discriminate aetiology (BMJ Best Practice: Childhood pneumonia).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Pneumonia in children

options.text: Dense lobar consolidation with air bronchograms on a focal CXR
options.rationale: Lobar consolidation is associated with bacterial pneumonia but radiographic appearance is imperfect on its own; viral pneumonia can occasionally produce lobar patterns and bacterial pneumonia can be patchy. A biomarker pattern is more specific (RCH CPG: CAP; BMJ Best Practice).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Pneumonia in children

options.text: An isolated CRP of 124 mg/L without any other supporting features
options.rationale: CRP rises in both viral and bacterial illness; an isolated CRP of 124 mg/L is suggestive but has worse specificity for bacterial pneumonia than procalcitonin with a neutrophilic leucocytosis (BMJ Best Practice).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Pneumonia in children

options.text: Procalcitonin of 2.4 microg/L with neutrophil-predominant leucocytosis on the FBC
options.rationale: A procalcitonin above approximately 0.5 microg/L (and especially above 2) combined with neutrophilic leucocytosis is the most specific biomarker pattern for bacterial pneumonia; isolated CRP elevation and lobar appearance both have meaningful overlap with severe viral pneumonia. The combined pattern strengthens the bacterial diagnosis substantially (BMJ Best Practice; Stockmann 2018 J Pediatr).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Pneumonia in children

options.text: Symptom duration of 4 days, sibling with coryzal illness at home
options.rationale: Both bacterial and viral pneumonia commonly follow a viral URTI; sibling contact is non-discriminating and short symptom duration favours neither (BMJ Best Practice).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Pneumonia in children

explanation.summary: No single feature reliably distinguishes bacterial from viral paediatric pneumonia. The combination of procalcitonin elevation (especially >0.5 microg/L), neutrophilic leucocytosis, lobar consolidation on CXR and high fever is more specific than any single feature. In practice, all children with clinical pneumonia receive empirical antibiotics in Australia, but biomarker stratification informs duration and de-escalation.
key_points: Bacterial features: high fever, focal/lobar consolidation, neutrophilia, elevated PCT
Viral features: diffuse interstitial pattern, lymphocytosis, normal PCT, prominent URTI
Procalcitonin >0.5 microg/L is the best single biomarker for bacterial cause
Treat clinical pneumonia empirically; reserve narrow-spectrum oral amoxicillin first
Common pathogens by age: <2 viral, 2-5 viral plus pneumococcus, >5 add mycoplasma
explanation.pearls: Procalcitonin is more specific than CRP for bacterial infection in children but is not universally available; combined with neutrophilia and focal imaging it is the most powerful discriminator.

sources.label: BMJ Best Practice: Pneumonia in children

stem: A 22-month-old boy is brought to ED by his grandmother 3 hours after a sudden coughing and choking episode while eating peanuts on the lounge floor. He had a few minutes of distress and gagging that settled, but since then he has had an intermittent dry cough and noisy breathing. He is alert and playful in the ED, T 36.9 deg C, RR 32, HR 124, SpO2 97% on room air. He has no respiratory distress at rest and no stridor. Auscultation reveals reduced breath sounds and a focal monophonic wheeze over the right side, with normal air entry on the left. There is no recession at rest. He has no history of recurrent wheeze and has never been diagnosed with asthma. There is no fever, drooling, or systemic illness. An inspiratory CXR appears unremarkable with symmetrical lung volumes and no focal opacity.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9 deg C, RR 32, HR 124, SpO2 97% on room air.
data_table.Examination: Alert, playful. Reduced breath sounds and focal monophonic wheeze on the right. No stridor. No recession.
data_table.Investigations: Inspiratory CXR unremarkable, symmetrical lung volumes.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Discharge home with safety-netting given the normal CXR and absence of acute distress
options.rationale: Normal CXR does not exclude a radiolucent foreign body (such as peanut); a witnessed choking episode followed by focal asymmetrical findings has a high pre-test probability that warrants definitive evaluation (RCH CPG: Foreign body aspiration; BMJ Best Practice).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Foreign body aspiration

options.text: Trial salbutamol 6 puffs via spacer and review in 1 hour for response
options.rationale: A focal monophonic wheeze in a toddler with a clear choking history is not asthma; bronchodilator trial delays definitive diagnosis and is not standard of care (RCH CPG: Foreign body aspiration).

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg and oral prednisolone for post-aspiration pneumonitis empirically
options.rationale: There is no fever, no consolidation and no systemic illness; empirical antibiotics and steroids do not address the retained foreign body and may delay bronchoscopy (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Chest CT to confirm presence and location of foreign body before intervention
options.rationale: CT is more sensitive than CXR but exposes a young child to non-trivial radiation and rarely changes management because rigid bronchoscopy remains the diagnostic and therapeutic standard; CT is reserved for atypical presentations (RCH CPG: Foreign body aspiration).

options.text: Inspiratory and expiratory chest radiographs and refer for urgent rigid bronchoscopy now
options.rationale: A witnessed choking episode followed by focal monophonic wheeze and asymmetrical breath sounds is the classic triad of foreign body aspiration. Inspiratory/expiratory or lateral decubitus films may show air trapping on the affected side, but a normal CXR does not exclude FBA; rigid bronchoscopy under GA is both diagnostic and therapeutic and is the standard next step (RCH CPG: Foreign body aspiration; BMJ Best Practice).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Foreign body aspiration

explanation.summary: Foreign body aspiration classically presents in toddlers with a witnessed choking episode followed by focal monophonic wheeze and reduced breath sounds on the affected side. A normal CXR does not exclude the diagnosis. Inspiratory/expiratory views may show air trapping; rigid bronchoscopy under GA remains both diagnostic and therapeutic.
key_points: Classic triad: choke + cough + focal wheeze / reduced breath sounds
Most common location: right main bronchus (more vertical takeoff)
Normal CXR does NOT rule out FBA
Inspiratory/expiratory or decubitus films may show air trapping
Rigid bronchoscopy is the gold standard
explanation.pearls: If the history is right, take the child to theatre even with a normal CXR. Peanuts and other organic foreign bodies are radiolucent and frequently missed.

sources.label: BMJ Best Practice: Foreign body aspiration

stem: A 3-year-old boy is reviewed at the GP after his third episode of viral-triggered wheeze in 12 months. Each episode has lasted around 3 to 5 days, has been associated with a clear upper respiratory tract infection and has responded to salbutamol via spacer with mask within 24 to 48 hours. None of the episodes have required oral steroids, ED presentation or hospital admission. He has been completely asymptomatic between episodes, with no nocturnal cough, no exercise-induced wheeze and no daytime symptoms when he is not unwell with a virus. There is no personal history of eczema or food allergy. Family history is significant for an older sibling with allergic rhinitis but no asthma. His parents ask whether he should be on a daily inhaled corticosteroid because his older cousin is on one. On examination he is well, has a normal chest examination, height and weight on the 60th centile, and is fully immunised.

data_table.Vital signs: Asymptomatic at review. T 36.8 deg C, RR 22, HR 100, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Investigations: Symptom-free between three viral-triggered episodes in last 12 months. No interval symptoms. No atopy.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Commence daily low-dose inhaled corticosteroid (fluticasone propionate 50 microg twice daily) via spacer with mask
options.rationale: Daily ICS is reserved for children with frequent symptoms, multiple severe attacks or interval symptoms - typically a Modified Asthma Predictive Index pattern or more than 2 to 3 attacks per year with severity features. This child has infrequent viral-triggered wheeze without interval symptoms (National Asthma Council Handbook; RCH CPG: Asthma).
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

options.text: Commence a daily oral leukotriene receptor antagonist (montelukast 4 mg) as maintenance
options.rationale: Montelukast has modest benefit in pre-school wheeze but is not preferred first-line maintenance over ICS when maintenance is indicated, and is not indicated here; it also carries an FDA black-box neuropsychiatric warning (National Asthma Council Handbook).
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

options.text: Continue PRN salbutamol via spacer for episodes and provide a written action plan
options.rationale: Episodic viral-triggered wheeze in a child under 5 without interval symptoms, without atopy, and with fewer than 3 to 4 severe attacks per year is managed with PRN salbutamol via MDI plus spacer with mask, technique reinforcement and a written action plan. Maintenance ICS is not indicated until episodes are more frequent, more severe or interval symptoms develop (National Asthma Council Handbook; RCH CPG: Asthma).
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

options.text: Commence pre-emptive high-dose inhaled corticosteroid at the onset of each upper respiratory tract infection
options.rationale: High-dose pre-emptive ICS at URTI onset has some evidence in severe pre-school wheezers but is not standard first-line; risks include growth velocity reduction and rebound on cessation (National Asthma Council Handbook).
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

options.text: Refer to a paediatric respiratory specialist for assessment and formal lung function testing
options.rationale: Specialist referral is reserved for diagnostic uncertainty, severe or frequent attacks, failure of standard therapy or atypical features; this is a textbook pre-school viral wheeze presentation and can be managed in primary care (National Asthma Council Handbook).
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

explanation.summary: Pre-school wheeze in children under 5 is divided into episodic viral-triggered wheeze (most common, often resolves) and multi-trigger wheeze (more likely to evolve into asthma). Maintenance ICS is indicated when attacks are frequent (more than 2 to 3 per year with severity features), when interval symptoms are present, or when there are strong atopic features. PRN salbutamol via spacer with mask and a written action plan is the cornerstone of episodic management.
key_points: Episodic viral wheeze without interval symptoms: PRN salbutamol
Maintenance ICS only if >2-3 attacks/year, interval symptoms or atopy
Use mask spacer in children under 4-5 unless mouthpiece technique reliable
Provide a written action plan for every wheezy child
Modified Asthma Predictive Index helps identify higher-risk patterns
explanation.pearls: Most viral-triggered pre-school wheeze resolves by school age. Avoid starting ICS in children who do not meet maintenance criteria - growth effects accumulate.

sources.label: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

stem: A 2-year-old girl presents at 2030 on Friday with 24 hours of a barking cough and hoarse cry that has worsened over the evening. She has had a 2-day coryzal illness. Her father reports increasing work of breathing in the last hour and audible noise on breathing in. In the resuscitation cubicle she is sitting upright on her father's lap, anxious, and refusing to drink. She has continuous inspiratory stridor at rest with marked sternal recession and tracheal tug. There is no drooling, she is afebrile at 37.7 deg C, SpO2 95 percent on room air, RR 36, HR 142. Her cry is hoarse but she can speak in short phrases. She is fully immunised including Hib, with no atopy and no history suggestive of foreign body aspiration. Air entry is symmetrical without focal crackles or wheeze. She has had no previous croup admissions or recurrent stridor between viral illnesses. Weight 13 kg. The clinician calculates a Westley score of 6.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.7 deg C, RR 36, HR 142, SpO2 95% on room air.
data_table.Examination: Continuous stridor at rest. Marked sternal recession and tracheal tug. No drooling. Hoarse but phonating.
data_table.Investigations: Westley score 6 (moderate). No CXR. Weight 13 kg.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate combination of immediate management and minimum observation?

options.text: Oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg alone, discharge after 1-hour observation if stable
options.rationale: Dexamethasone alone is appropriate for mild croup (Westley 0-2); a moderate score (3-7) with stridor at rest and marked recession requires nebulised adrenaline plus minimum 4 hours observation for rebound.

options.text: Nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1:1000 plus oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg (or prednisolone if no oral access), observe minimum 4 hours after the last adrenaline dose before considering discharge
options.rationale: Moderate croup (Westley 3-7) with stridor at rest is treated with nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1:1000 and oral dexamethasone (0.15-0.6 mg/kg). Adrenaline acts within 30 minutes but lasts about 2 hours; steroid onset is 1-2 hours. The 4-hour minimum observation captures any rebound, per RCH CPG: Croup.

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg and urgent ENT for suspected epiglottitis
options.rationale: Epiglottitis presents with drooling, tripod posture, toxic appearance and muffled (not hoarse) voice in an unimmunised child; this is typical viral croup.

options.text: Heliox via face mask and humidified oxygen; defer dexamethasone pending ENT review
options.rationale: Humidified air and heliox have no proven benefit; delaying steroid is harmful because steroid reduces return visits and admission.

options.text: Nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg, ipratropium 250 microgram and oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg
options.rationale: That is acute asthma treatment; beta-agonists do not address subglottic oedema in croup.

explanation.summary: Moderate croup (Westley 3 to 7; stridor at rest with recession but otherwise alert and pink) is treated with nebulised adrenaline (5 mL of 1:1000) plus oral dexamethasone (0.15 mg/kg standard, up to 0.6 mg/kg) and observed for at least 4 hours after the last adrenaline dose to detect rebound. Severe croup (Westley 8 or above, hypoxia, exhaustion, drowsiness) needs PICU and consideration of intubation.
key_points: Mild (Westley 0-2): dex 0.15 mg/kg PO, observe briefly
Moderate (3-7): nebulised adrenaline + dex, observe 4 hrs post-adrenaline
Severe (8+): adrenaline + dex, ICU, anaesthetics, consider intubation
Adrenaline acts in 10-30 min, effect lasts ~2 hrs; rebound mandates observation
Steroid onset ~1-2 hrs; covers symptom course of 24-48 hrs
explanation.pearls: Drooling, tripod posture, muffled voice and toxic appearance in an unwell unimmunised child should prompt consideration of epiglottitis or bacterial tracheitis rather than croup, with airway team activation rather than examination of the throat.

stem: A 10-year-old girl with asthma has been reviewed in the GP clinic with her mother for a routine 3-monthly follow-up. She has been on fluticasone propionate 100 microg twice daily (low-dose ICS) for 8 months. Over the last 3 months she has had daytime wheeze or cough on most days, has woken at night with cough at least once a week, and has needed her salbutamol on average 4 to 5 days per week including before sport. There have been no oral steroid courses in this period and no hospital presentations. The GP has watched her use her MDI and spacer (mouthpiece) in clinic and the technique is excellent. Adherence on Webster pack review is 90% and the parents confirm she takes it morning and night. There are no environmental triggers identified - no smoking in the household, no pets, and no recent changes in housing. Spirometry today shows FEV1 78% predicted and FEV1/FVC 0.74 with significant bronchodilator reversibility of 14%.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7 deg C, RR 18, HR 92, SpO2 99% on room air.
data_table.Examination: Well, normal chest examination. Growth on 50th centile.
data_table.Investigations: Spirometry: FEV1 78% predicted, FEV1/FVC 0.74, bronchodilator reversibility 14%. Adherence 90%, technique correct.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in her controller therapy?

options.text: Continue current low-dose ICS and add daily oral montelukast 5 mg as maintenance add-on
options.rationale: Montelukast is an alternative or add-on option but is less effective than adding a LABA at this step in children 6 and over; the National Asthma Council prefers ICS-LABA combination as the preferred step-up (National Asthma Council Handbook).
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

options.text: Step up to combination ICS plus LABA (fluticasone-salmeterol 100/50 microg twice daily)
options.rationale: She has poor symptom control on low-dose ICS despite correct technique, good adherence and no modifiable factors. The National Asthma Council preferred step-up in children 6 and over is combination low-dose ICS-LABA. Doubling the ICS dose is an alternative but ICS-LABA generally produces better symptom and lung function gains for the same total ICS exposure (National Asthma Council Handbook; GINA 2024).
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

options.text: Stop the regular ICS and commence as-needed budesonide-formoterol via MDI plus spacer
options.rationale: As-needed ICS-formoterol (the MART approach) is recommended in adolescents and adults at certain steps but is not the preferred maintenance pathway for a 10-year-old who is already on regular ICS with poor control; step-up to combination ICS-LABA is preferred (National Asthma Council Handbook).
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

options.text: Refer to a paediatric respiratory specialist for assessment for omalizumab or biologic therapy
options.rationale: Biologic therapy is reserved for severe, uncontrolled asthma despite high-dose ICS-LABA plus adherence optimisation, and is a tertiary-care decision; she has not yet had a step-up from low-dose ICS (National Asthma Council Handbook).
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

options.text: Add a daily long-acting muscarinic antagonist such as tiotropium via Respimat inhaler
options.rationale: LAMA is an add-on for severe asthma uncontrolled on medium- to high-dose ICS-LABA; it is not the first step-up from low-dose ICS in a child (National Asthma Council Handbook).
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

explanation.summary: For a school-age child with uncontrolled asthma on low-dose ICS despite confirmed correct technique, good adherence and absence of modifiable triggers, the National Asthma Council preferred step-up is combination low-dose ICS-LABA. Doubling the ICS dose is an acceptable alternative. Always recheck adherence, technique, environmental factors and the diagnosis before stepping up.
key_points: Before stepping up: recheck technique, adherence, triggers, diagnosis (the 4 As)
Preferred step-up (age 6+): low-dose ICS-LABA
Alternative: medium-dose ICS, or ICS + LTRA
Step down once control achieved for 3 months
Refer to specialist if uncontrolled on medium-dose ICS-LABA
explanation.pearls: The most common reason for 'uncontrolled asthma' is technique or adherence failure, not severity. Watch every patient use their inhaler at every review.

sources.label: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

stem: A 7-week-old previously well boy is brought to ED by his mother after 10 days of a worsening cough. The cough started as a non-specific coryzal illness with mild rhinorrhoea but over the last 5 days has become severely paroxysmal, with episodes of coughing lasting 30 to 60 seconds followed by vomiting and a sharp inspiratory sound. Between paroxysms he is settled and appears well. He has had two witnessed brief apnoeic episodes at home, each lasting less than 15 seconds, with no colour change. He is bottle-fed and taking only about 80% of his usual volume. He is not yet immunised because his mother declined the antenatal pertussis booster and the 6-week vaccines were missed. His mother has had a 3-week non-productive cough that she has attributed to a viral illness. On examination he is afebrile at 36.9 deg C, RR 42, HR 158, SpO2 97% on room air, with mild intercostal recession and unremarkable auscultation between paroxysms. FBC shows white cell count 28 x10^9/L with a marked lymphocytosis. CXR shows perihilar peribronchial thickening with no focal consolidation. A nasopharyngeal pertussis PCR has been sent.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9 deg C, RR 42, HR 158, SpO2 97% on room air.
data_table.Examination: Settled between paroxysms. Mild recession. Auscultation clear between coughs.
data_table.Investigations: FBC: WCC 28 x10^9/L, marked lymphocytosis. CXR: perihilar peribronchial thickening. Pertussis PCR sent.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate combination of management steps?

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 25 mg/kg every 8 hours for 5 days, discharge home with safety-netting
options.rationale: Amoxicillin does not cover Bordetella pertussis; azithromycin is the macrolide of choice. Pertussis in an infant under 6 months with apnoea warrants admission for observation, not discharge, and is notifiable on clinical suspicion (Therapeutic Guidelines; CDNA Pertussis SoNG).

options.text: Oral azithromycin 10 mg/kg daily for 5 days plus salbutamol nebs, no public health action
options.rationale: Azithromycin and admission are appropriate but salbutamol is not effective for pertussis bronchospasm and contact tracing plus household post-exposure prophylaxis is mandatory under CDNA guidelines (CDNA Pertussis SoNG).

options.text: Admit for monitoring, oral azithromycin for 5 days, notify public health, household chemoprophylaxis
options.rationale: Infants under 6 months with pertussis and any apnoeic features should be admitted because of the risk of bradycardia, apnoea and death. Azithromycin 10 mg/kg once daily for 5 days is the first-line macrolide for infants under 6 months (lower risk of pyloric stenosis than erythromycin). Pertussis is notifiable on clinical suspicion, and close household contacts (especially infants and pregnant women) require post-exposure prophylaxis with the same regimen (Therapeutic Guidelines; CDNA Pertussis SoNG; NCIRS).

options.text: Oral erythromycin 40 mg/kg/day in 4 doses for 14 days, no admission required
options.rationale: Erythromycin is associated with pyloric stenosis in infants under 1 month and has more GI side effects than azithromycin; admission of an under-6-month-old with apnoea is recommended regardless of SpO2 (Therapeutic Guidelines; CDNA Pertussis SoNG).

options.text: IV ceftriaxone with oral prednisolone, defer notification until pertussis PCR confirms
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone and prednisolone do not treat pertussis; pertussis is notifiable on clinical suspicion to permit timely contact tracing and prophylaxis, not after laboratory confirmation (CDNA Pertussis SoNG).

explanation.summary: Pertussis in unvaccinated young infants presents in three phases (catarrhal, paroxysmal, convalescent) and carries significant morbidity from apnoea, pneumonia and pertussis-related pulmonary hypertension. Infants under 6 months with apnoea or feeding difficulty are admitted. Azithromycin 10 mg/kg daily for 5 days is the first-line treatment in young infants (lower pyloric stenosis risk than erythromycin). Pertussis is notifiable on clinical suspicion and close contacts require post-exposure prophylaxis with the same macrolide.
key_points: Three phases: catarrhal (1-2 wk) - paroxysmal (2-6 wk) - convalescent (weeks)
Infants <6 months: admit if apnoea, feeding difficulty, cyanosis
Azithromycin first-line in young infants (10 mg/kg/day x 5 days)
Notifiable disease on clinical suspicion
Post-exposure prophylaxis for close contacts including pregnant women and young infants
explanation.pearls: Lymphocytosis (often >20 x10^9/L) is a clinical clue in pertussis. In ex-premature or under-6-month infants, the first sign may be apnoea rather than cough.

stem: An 11-year-old girl is reviewed in the GP clinic 2 weeks after a moderate asthma exacerbation that required oral prednisolone in ED but no admission. She is on fluticasone-salmeterol 100/50 microgram twice daily and PRN salbutamol via MDI plus spacer. Technique is excellent and adherence is good. Over the past year she has had three exacerbations, each beginning with several days of increased salbutamol use and mild nocturnal cough; on two occasions her mother gave a single dose of salbutamol and waited overnight before review, and the girl ended up needing oral prednisolone. The clinician is now writing a National Asthma Council action plan and wants the family to act earlier. The school has the previous plan but it is 18 months old. The family lives 15 minutes from the nearest ED.

data_table.Vital signs: Asymptomatic at review. T 36.6 deg C, RR 16, HR 84, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Investigations: Three exacerbations in 12 months, two required oral prednisolone. Technique and adherence good.

lead_in: Which yellow-zone instruction set is most appropriate to write into her plan?

options.text: Continue preventer at current dose; salbutamol 2 puffs every 4 hours; no other change
options.rationale: Yellow zone requires intensification; 2 puffs every 4 hours is the same as background PRN dosing and will not catch the early deterioration pattern seen in her past exacerbations.
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

options.text: Self-initiate a 3-day course of oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg at home at first sign of worsening before any clinician contact
options.rationale: Home self-initiated prednisolone is reserved for selected children with frequent severe exacerbations under specialist guidance and a tailored plan; not routine yellow-zone advice in primary care.
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

options.text: Stop the preventer and rely on salbutamol alone until the worsening symptoms resolve
options.rationale: Stopping the preventer during a deterioration worsens control; preventer should be continued or temporarily intensified.
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

options.text: Salbutamol 4-6 puffs via spacer every 4 hours; temporarily increase preventer (e.g. quadruple ICS or as specified in her plan); GP review within 24-48 hours; clear red-flag escalation criteria (no relief from salbutamol, severe breathlessness, blue lips, inability to speak in sentences) prompting call-000 or ED
options.rationale: The National Asthma Council yellow zone increases salbutamol to 4-6 puffs via spacer every 4 hours, continues or temporarily steps up preventer, and arranges GP review within 24-48 hours with clear red-flag criteria for ED. Written zone-based plans reduce hospitalisation and time off school.
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

options.text: Present immediately to ED for any increase in salbutamol use regardless of severity
options.rationale: Routine ED presentation for any salbutamol increase produces unnecessary attendances and undermines self-management; clear thresholds for home vs GP vs ED are the purpose of the action plan.
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

explanation.summary: A written asthma action plan uses traffic-light zones: green (well, take preventer), yellow (symptoms worsening - intensify), red (severe - emergency). Yellow zone actions include increased salbutamol (4 to 6 puffs every 4 hours via spacer), continued or temporarily increased preventer, and review within 24 to 48 hours. Red zone triggers emergency response (4 puffs salbutamol, repeat every 4 minutes if no improvement, call 000).
key_points: Green: regular preventer + reliever as needed; recognise triggers
Yellow: salbutamol 4-6 puffs q4h; continue or increase preventer; GP review 24-48 hrs
Red: 4 puffs every 4 min, call 000, oral prednisolone if prescribed
Update plan every 12 months and after every significant exacerbation
Provide a copy to the school and any out-of-school carer
explanation.pearls: Action plans reduce hospitalisations and school absence. The most common reason for repeated exacerbations is delayed family escalation in the yellow zone.

sources.label: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

stem: A 12-year-old girl with cystic fibrosis (homozygous F508del) is admitted with a 7-day history of worsening productive cough, increased sputum volume that has become darker green and more tenacious than her usual baseline, reduced exercise tolerance with breathlessness climbing one flight of stairs at school, anorexia and 1.5 kg of weight loss over the previous 2 weeks despite her usual enzyme dosing. Her baseline FEV1 is 78% predicted; today it has fallen to 61% predicted on bedside spirometry, the largest single-visit drop she has had since starting her current modulator. She is fully adherent to her usual regimen of nebulised hypertonic saline 6%, dornase alfa, pancreatic enzyme replacement and twice-daily airway clearance physiotherapy with a positive expiratory pressure device. She is on elexacaftor-tezacaftor-ivacaftor and has been stable for the last 18 months. Her last sputum culture 6 weeks ago grew mucoid Pseudomonas aeruginosa sensitive to tobramycin, ceftazidime, piperacillin-tazobactam, and meropenem, and methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus sensitive to flucloxacillin. She has no penicillin allergy and her baseline serum creatinine 6 weeks ago was 55 micromol/L. On examination she is alert, T 37.8 deg C, RR 26, HR 110, SpO2 93% on room air. Auscultation reveals coarse crackles at both upper lobes and the right middle lobe with widespread expiratory wheeze. CXR shows bilateral upper-lobe bronchiectasis with new patchy consolidation at the right middle lobe compared to her baseline. A sputum culture has been sent on arrival.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.8 deg C, RR 26, HR 110, SpO2 93% on room air.
data_table.Examination: Alert. Coarse crackles both upper lobes + RML. Widespread expiratory wheeze. Cachectic appearance.
data_table.Investigations: FEV1 61% (baseline 78%). Last sputum: mucoid Pseudomonas (sensitive to tobramycin, ceftazidime, pip-taz, meropenem); MSSA. New CXR consolidation at RML.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate empirical IV antibiotic regimen pending fresh sputum culture results?

options.text: Oral ciprofloxacin 20 mg/kg twice daily for 14 days as outpatient antipseudomonal monotherapy with home physiotherapy
options.rationale: Oral ciprofloxacin monotherapy is used for early Pseudomonas eradication or mild outpatient exacerbations; an FEV1 drop of 17 percentage points with new consolidation, weight loss and hypoxia warrants IV dual therapy with admission (Cystic Fibrosis Foundation Pulmonary Guidelines).

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg once daily plus oral azithromycin 10 mg/kg once daily for 14 days
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone does not reliably cover Pseudomonas aeruginosa; ceftazidime, piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime or meropenem is required. Azithromycin has anti-inflammatory effects in CF but is not adequate antipseudomonal therapy (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: IV flucloxacillin 50 mg/kg every 6 hours plus oral rifampicin as targeted antistaphylococcal combination therapy
options.rationale: Flucloxacillin targets MSSA, which is reasonable additional cover but does not address the chronic Pseudomonas that is far more likely to be driving this exacerbation given her sputum history (Cystic Fibrosis Foundation Pulmonary Guidelines).

options.text: IV meropenem 40 mg/kg every 8 hours as broad-spectrum antipseudomonal monotherapy for 14 days
options.rationale: Carbapenem monotherapy is reserved for multi-resistant Pseudomonas or beta-lactam allergy; standard practice in a child with sensitive mucoid Pseudomonas is a beta-lactam plus aminoglycoside combination, preserving carbapenems for resistant strains (Therapeutic Guidelines; CF Australia Standards of Care).
source_refs: Cystic Fibrosis Australia Standards of Care

options.text: IV ceftazidime 50 mg/kg every 8 hours plus IV tobramycin 10 mg/kg once daily, alongside intensified airway clearance physiotherapy
options.rationale: A CF pulmonary exacerbation in an adolescent with chronic mucoid Pseudomonas is treated empirically with dual anti-pseudomonal IV therapy combining an antipseudomonal beta-lactam (commonly ceftazidime) with an aminoglycoside (tobramycin once daily, with levels monitored) for 10 to 14 days. Intensified airway clearance physiotherapy and nutritional optimisation are essential adjuncts and her CFTR modulator is continued. MSSA is co-covered or treated separately if persistent (Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory; CF Australia Standards of Care; Cystic Fibrosis Foundation Guidelines).
source_refs: Cystic Fibrosis Australia Standards of Care

explanation.summary: A CF pulmonary exacerbation is defined by increased cough, sputum volume or purulence, dyspnoea, anorexia, weight loss, and a fall in FEV1 (typically 10% or more from baseline). Admission is indicated for significant FEV1 decline, hypoxia or failure to respond to oral therapy. Standard empirical IV regimen for chronic mucoid Pseudomonas: a beta-lactam (ceftazidime or piperacillin-tazobactam) plus an aminoglycoside (tobramycin once daily) for 10 to 14 days, with intensified physiotherapy and dietetic input. CFTR modulators are continued throughout.
key_points: Exacerbation triggers: fall in FEV1 >=10%, increased sputum/cough, weight loss, hypoxia
Empirical IV: anti-pseudomonal beta-lactam + aminoglycoside x 10-14 days
Once-daily tobramycin with trough monitoring (renal and auditory toxicity)
Continue CFTR modulator and adjust based on culture
Adjuncts: intensified physio, nutrition, treat any DIOS or hypoglycaemia
explanation.pearls: In a CF patient with chronic Pseudomonas, never treat an exacerbation with a single agent. The double-cover principle reduces emergence of resistance and is the Australian and international standard.

sources.label: Cystic Fibrosis Australia Standards of Care
